text: The surgeon performs a robotic low anterior resection in a female patient with high BMI. A sponge is used to keep the small bowel away from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle. Medial dissection is underway to access the superior hemorrhoidal artery, with challenges in identifying the retrorectal plane due to excess fatty tissue.

The surgeon performs a robotic low anterior resection in a female patient with high BMI, using a sponge to keep the small bowel away from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle. Medial dissection is underway to access the superior hemorrhoidal artery, with challenges in finding the retrorectal plane due to excess fatty tissue.

The surgeon struggles with retraction as the sigmoid colon flops over the dissection area. Using hand-over-hand technique, the surgeon elevates the IMA pedicle and repositions the sponge to isolate the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle amidst excess fatty tissue. Switching to a vessel sealer for better hemostasis, the surgeon dissects through the fatty tissue, reaching the confluence of the superior hemorrhoidal and left colic vessels. The left ureter is identified beneath retroperitoneal fat before proceeding to transect the pedicle.

The surgeon struggles with retraction as the sigmoid colon flops over the dissection area. They perform hand-over-hand maneuvers to elevate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, repositioning the sponge to assist. The surgeon isolates the IMA superior hemorrhoidal pedicle amidst excess fatty tissue, switching to a vessel sealer for more secure and hemostatic dissection.

The surgeon approaches the confluence of the superior hemorrhoidal and left colic arteries, ensuring the left ureter is identified and safely positioned away from the mesenteric branches. The ureter is visible beneath retroperitoneal fat. The surgeon proceeds with dissection through the pedicle, confirming the ureter's position. The descending colon and sigmoid colon are shown during the dissection, with prior mobilization around the splenic flexure using the same ports without redocking.

The ureter is visible again as the surgeon performs medial dissection. The descending colon and sigmoid colon are shown during the dissection. The surgeon has mobilized around the splenic flexure, although this step is not shown in the video.

The surgeon suspends the uterus and right ovary with sutures to clear the surgical field, then performs a right mesorectal excision dissection. The dissection progresses posteriorly, navigating through excess fatty tissue, and continues down the left side. The surgeon sets up to dissect across the anterior portion, targeting the rectovaginal septum.

The surgeon suspends the uterus and right ovary with sutures to clear the surgical field, then proceeds with the right mesorectal excision dissection. The dissection is performed in a posterior direction, navigating through an excess of fatty tissue in the plane.

The surgeon proceeds with dissection down the left side, setting up to cross the anterior portion and entering the rectovaginal septum. The dissection is performed circumferentially, navigating through the tissue planes to reach the desired level for the total mesorectal excision (TME).
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection around the rectum, navigating through challenging tissue planes to progress the total mesorectal excision (TME) in a patient with high BMI.

The surgeon checks the location of the tumor to ensure the dissection is beneath it. Using a vessel sealer, the colonic and rectal mesentery are thinned before deploying a 45-load stapler into the deep pelvis. Two loads are fired to transect the rectum just above the pelvic diaphragm, preparing for a stapled anastomosis. The proximal colon mesentery is taken, and 26 lymph nodes are retrieved during the dissection.

The surgeon checks the location of the tumor to ensure the dissection is beneath it. Using a vessel sealer, the colonic and rectal mesentery are thinned out before firing a 45-load stapler into the deep pelvis. Two loads are used to staple across the rectum just above the pelvic diaphragm, preparing for a stapled anastomosis with approximately three to four centimeters of rectum remaining beneath the anastomosis.

The surgeon checks perfusion under ICG mode after completing the dissection, which yielded twenty-six lymph nodes. The colon margin is prepared for exteriorization, ensuring the colonic mesentery remains intact without needing additional energy devices.

The surgeon checks perfusion under ICG mode and marks the charred portion of the colon for removal. The stapled end is exteriorized through a Pfannenstiel incision. The anvil is placed, and the surgeon prepares for anastomosis by mating the two ends using the EEA stapler, then returns to the robotic console to tighten the EEA.

The surgeon marks the charred portion of the colon for removal and exteriorizes the stapled end through a Pfannenstiel incision. The anvil is placed, and the surgeon mates the two ends using an EEA stapler, performing the anastomosis. A rigid scope is used to check for bleeding, and a leak test confirms the anastomosis is widely patent, located approximately three centimeters from the anal verge.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis, setting it approximately three centimeters from the anal verge. A rigid scope is used to confirm the absence of bleeding and leaks, showing the anastomosis is widely patent. The patient's body habitus allows for non-diversion, and postoperative recovery is noted to be successful.
